entity.name: Center for Security and Emerging Technology (CSET)
entity.description: Georgetown-housed research center; the most-cited AI policy shop in DC. Runs the primary public datasets on AI talent flows, chip controls effectiveness, and US/China AI competition. Their analysis explicitly shapes BIS export-control rulemaking and Hill briefings on compute thresholds and foreign-talent policy.
aliases: Center for Security and Emerging Technology

activity.title: Full-Spectrum Propaganda in the Social Media Era
activity.raw_text: In a new Security Studies article, Renee DiResta and Josh A. Goldstein lay out how state-backed propagandists run "full-spectrum" propaganda campaigns, relying on overt and covert tools across broadcast and social media. Abstract: How do states wage propaganda campaigns in the social media era? Existing studies often investigate either covert fake accounts on social media platforms or overt propaganda on traditional broadcast media. We argue that states now wage integrated campaigns, using multiple media channels that cite each other directly, or parallel campaigns, using multiple channels concurrently but independently of one another.

enrichment.summary: CSET-affiliated researchers Renee DiResta and Josh A. Goldstein published a Security Studies article examining how states conduct "full-spectrum" propaganda campaigns in the social media era. The paper argues that modern state propaganda is no longer limited to either covert fake accounts or overt broadcast media, but instead combines both through integrated or parallel multi-channel campaigns. Integrated campaigns involve media channels that directly cite one another, while parallel campaigns use multiple channels concurrently but independently.

activity.title: CSET Director Helen Toner Testifies Before Senate Judiciary Committee
activity.raw_text: Washington, D.C. (April 22, 2026) — This morning, Helen Toner, Interim Executive Director at Georgetown University's Center for Security and Emerging Technology (CSET), testified before the U.S. Senate Committee on the Judiciary. The hearing, "Stealth Stealing: China's Ongoing Theft of U.S. Innovation," examined policy solutions to maintain U.S. technological leadership and strengthen U.S. intellectual property (IP) protections. As competition with China continues to escalate, Toner's testimony provided recommendations for bolstering security and countering threats while maintaining U.S. advantage.

_verify_phrases: Stealth Stealing: China's Ongoing Theft of U.S. Innovation

enrichment.summary: Helen Toner, Interim Executive Director of CSET, testified before the U.S. Senate Committee on the Judiciary at a hearing titled "Stealth Stealing: China's Ongoing Theft of U.S. Innovation." The testimony addressed policy solutions to maintain U.S. technological leadership and strengthen intellectual property protections in the context of escalating competition with China. Toner provided recommendations for bolstering security and countering threats while preserving U.S. advantage in technology.
enrichment.stance_quote: Toner's testimony provided recommendations for bolstering security and countering threats while maintaining U.S. advantage.

activity.title: Physical AI: A Primer for Policymakers on AI-Robotics Convergence
activity.raw_text: John VerWey, "Physical AI: A Primer for Policymakers on AI-Robotics Convergence," Center for Security and Emerging Technology, February 2026, https://doi.org/10.51593/20250023. This paper builds on previous CSET research looking at the robotics patent landscape to characterize competitiveness using CSET's Map of Science and separate research that proposed a methodology for identifying and characterizing an emerging technology.

_verify_phrases: Physical AI: A Primer for Policymakers on AI-Robotics Convergence

enrichment.summary: CSET published a policy primer on "Physical AI," defined as the convergence of artificial intelligence and robotics, authored by John VerWey in February 2026. The paper draws on prior CSET research into the robotics patent landscape and a methodology for characterizing emerging technologies to help policymakers understand the competitive dynamics of this field. It uses CSET's Map of Science to assess competitiveness in AI-robotics convergence.

activity.title: When AI Builds AI: Findings From a Workshop on Automation of AI R&D
activity.raw_text: For decades, scientists have speculated about the possibility of machines that can improve themselves. Today, artificial intelligence (AI) systems are increasingly integral parts of the research pipeline at leading AI companies. Some observers see this as evidence that fully automated AI research and development (R&D) is on the way, potentially leading to a rapid acceleration of AI capabilities and impaired ability for humans to understand and control AI. Others see the use of AI for research as a mundane extension of existing software tools.

_verify_phrases: Findings From a Workshop on Automation of AI R&D

enrichment.summary: CSET published a policy paper summarizing findings from a workshop on the automation of AI research and development. The paper examines the degree to which AI systems are being used in the AI research pipeline at leading companies, and explores divergent views on whether this represents a path toward fully automated AI R&D or merely an incremental software tool extension. It addresses potential implications including rapid acceleration of AI capabilities and reduced human understanding and control of AI systems.
